_comment: Topic/type_id-level prerequisites for skill deps the section→chapter export drops or misattributes. Keys and requires are curriculum type_ids. Edit here, then: python scripts/export_prerequisite_browser.py

calc_diff_average_rates_of_change.reason: Average rate of change is pre-derivative setup; no calculus Limits chapter required.

calc_diff_definition_of_the_derivative.reason: The limit definition of the derivative is the skill that actually requires Limits (and average rates as the difference quotient).

calc_diff_instantaneous_rates_of_change.reason: Instantaneous rate is the derivative at a point; builds on the definition, not a fresh Limits chapter tour.

calc_diff_power_rule.reason: Power Rule is a differentiation method after the derivative concept is introduced; does not re-require Limits overview.

calc_diff_higher_order_derivatives.reason: Higher-order derivatives repeatedly apply basic differentiation rules (starting with Power Rule).

calc_diff_product_rule.reason: Product Rule extends basic differentiation; Power Rule fluency is assumed.

calc_diff_quotient_rule.reason: Quotient Rule builds on Power Rule and is closely related to Product Rule.

calc_diff_chain_rule.reason: Chain Rule composes basic differentiation methods (power/product/quotient).

calc_diff_rules_using_tables.reason: Table-based rule problems mix Power, Product, Quotient, and Chain.

calc_diff_trigonometric.reason: As generated: Easy is basic sin/cos/tan; Medium uses sin(kx) (chain with constant); Hard mixes product and chain. Not a Limits dependency.

calc_diff_inverse_trigonometric.reason: Inverse trig derivatives use trig derivative forms and the Chain Rule.

calc_diff_natural_logarithms_and_exponen.reason: Natural exp/log derivatives use basic rules and the Chain Rule.

calc_diff_other_base_logarithms_and_expo.reason: Other-base exp/log reduce to natural exp/log derivatives.

calc_diff_logarithmic.reason: Logarithmic differentiation combines log derivatives with Product/Chain.

calc_diff_implicit.reason: Implicit differentiation applies Product and Chain Rules to implicitly defined relations.

calc_diff_inverse_functions.reason: Inverse-function derivatives rely on the Chain Rule relationship.
